coredata.dc:title: A new insights into multicolor emissive carbon dots using Trachelospermum jasminoides leaves for the application of WLEDs
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Kumari, Rinki
Sahu, Sumanta Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have recently emerged as a very encouraging family of materials with augmented performance in WLEDs. Facile synthesis of CDs with high optical tuning using sustainable green precursors is a great challenge in the applications of WLEDs. In this work, we prepare red, green, and blue-color emissive CDs (RGB CDs) using an extract of Star Jasmine leaves (Trachelospermum jasminoides). The red emission originates due to the presence of an extensive conjugated carbonic system with a few esters (-COOR) and amide (-CONR) moieties on their surface. And the shorter wavelength emission appears due to the presence of carboxylic (-COO) moieties. Specifically, in green emissive CDs, the formation of the OZnO bond causes the fluorescence shifting (>100 nm) and a higher lifetime as it restricts the making of the conjugated -COOR/-CONR groups. The detailed study revealed that an average lifetime of CDs has a linear relationship with the core state lifetime. These highly stable and effective naturally produced RGB CDs have been used as fabrication of WLEDs, which exhibited CCT and CIE coordinates of 4283 K and (0.36,0.32), respectively. Additionally, the red CDs detected water and chloroform visually and fluorometrically in a broad range. This work will open up a new route for developing high-performance WLEDs using natural precursors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multicolor emission
White light emitting diodes
Solvent detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of red, blue, and green CDs
Effect of organic solvents on R2-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for the synthesis of R2-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs.
UV–VIS absorbance spectra of (a) R2-CDs, (c) G-CDs, and (e) B-CDs; and PLEx and PLEm of (b) R2-CDs, (d) G-CDs, and (f) B-CDs.
TEM images of (a) R2-CDs, (b) G-CDs, and (c) B-CDs; particle size histogram of (d) R2-CDs, (e) G-CDs, and (f) B-CDs.
(a) FTIR and (b) XPS spectrum of R2-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs.
(a-c) Raman spectrum and (d-f) lifetime decay plot of R2-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs.
CIE coordinate plot (a-d) and (e-h) their color spectrum for composites of (a) B-CDs/PU, G-CDs/PU, R2-CDs/PU, White color emissive/PU; (i) Blue LEDs, (j) Green LEDs, (k) Red LEDs, and (l) White LEDs.
Graph between F/F0 with the concentration of (a) Chloroform (0–70%, V/V) and (b) Water (0–70%, V/V).
Comparative table demonstrating precursors, synthesis condition, and application of existing leaves derived CDs.

head.ce:title: A new insights into multicolor emissive carbon dots using Trachelospermum jasminoides leaves for the application of WLEDs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad 826004, Jharkhand, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad 826004, Jharkhand, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have recently emerged as a very encouraging family of materials with augmented performance in WLEDs. Facile synthesis of CDs with high optical tuning using sustainable green precursors is a great challenge in the applications of WLEDs. In this work, we prepare red, green, and blue-color emissive CDs (RGB CDs) using an extract of Star Jasmine leaves (Trachelospermum jasminoides). The red emission originates due to the presence of an extensive conjugated carbonic system with a few esters (-COOR) and amide (-CONR) moieties on their surface. And the shorter wavelength emission appears due to the presence of carboxylic (-COO) moieties. Specifically, in green emissive CDs, the formation of the OZnO bond causes the fluorescence shifting (>100 nm) and a higher lifetime as it restricts the making of the conjugated -COOR/-CONR groups. The detailed study revealed that an average lifetime of CDs has a linear relationship with the core state lifetime. These highly stable and effective naturally produced RGB CDs have been used as fabrication of WLEDs, which exhibited CCT and CIE coordinates of 4283 K and (0.36,0.32), respectively. Additionally, the red CDs detected water and chloroform visually and fluorometrically in a broad range. This work will open up a new route for developing high-performance WLEDs using natural precursors.

ce:section.ce:para: WLEDs have demonstrated a significantly important role in lighting and displays because of their highly efficient, long-lifetime as well as low-power feeding. In general, two approaches have been reported in generating WLEDs, first is a device fabrication using a single chip coupled with different color phosphors. In this case, generally, a blue light-emitting chip coupled with green and red-light emitting phosphors to generates WLEDs. In the second one, blue, green, and red colored emitted chips combined as a sandwich in the layer-to-layer form to obtain WLEDs. Many researchers accepted these approaches and developed different phosphor materials for WLED applications. In general, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [1,2] perovskite QDs [3,4] and metal-organic frameworks [5–7] have been used as potential fluorophore material. For example, Li et al. have reported blue, green, and yellow QDs based on perovskite CsPbX3 by varying the content and class of anions [4]. Li and his coworkers have synthesized a series of Ln-MOFs via doping three rare earth metals Sm3+, Tb3+, and Dy3+ [6]. The white light has obtained by mounting Sm0.1Tb0.04 Dy0.06 MOF-based phosphor on a 389 nm GaN chip. In these above works, researchers have used either the rare-earth metal or heavy metal ion as starting materials for synthesizing the color converter. It is very well known that these types of starting materials are rare, expensive, and causes a toxic effect on human and the environment. Simultaneously, the complex synthesis process limits their large-scale production and applicability in WLEDs.

In comparison to the reported phosphorescent materials, CDs have been focused on as potential fluorophores in recent years [8–10]. For WLEDs application, numerous multicolor emissive CDs have been synthesized based on the organic compound by various approaches [11]. For example, Lee et al. have reported phenylenediamine-based blue, green, and red CDs exhibited emission at 445, 515, and 610 nm, respectively [12]. The multicolor emissive CDs are prepared using Tobias acid and o-phenylenediamine in three different solvents [13]. Alaş et al. have exhibited fluorescence tuning from 536 nm to 604 nm by varying the solvents from DCM to ethanol [14]. We have also reported pyromellitic acid [15,16] and 1,2,4-triaminobenzene dihydrochloride [17] based-multicolor emissive CDs for WLEDs. But these days, there has been a desire to alter the advanced research on natural precursors for WLEDs. These green precursors have additional advantages including high abundancy, non-toxicity, economical, large-scale production, and recycling of environmental wastes to value-added products [18,19]. Several CDs have been synthesized using leaves [20], fruits [21,22], vegetables [23], and environmental wastes [24], etc. Among natural sources, some plant leaves-based extract has originated as a promising natural precursor for synthesizing red-emissive CDs [25–27]. Kumawat et al. have conveyed the red fluorescence in Mango leaves are originated from the introduction of oxygen-containing functional groups [25]. Bhati et al. have shown the incorporation of Mg–N inside the graphitic carbonic system is the major reason behind red emission in Bougainvillea leaves [26]. To date, these red-emissive CDs have been synthesized and successfully applied in sensing, catalytic activity, and imaging only. The nonexistence of fluorescence tuning from a single natural source limits its applicability in the optoelectronics field. To date, multicolor emissive CDs from natural precursors have not been reported yet. Therefore, it is highly in demand to tune the fluorescence emission for the fabrication of WLEDs. The major constituents of leaves are organic acid, carbohydrates, essential oils, water, and minerals [28,29] which provide an excellent developed source of nitrogen and oxygen-conjugated carbon rings containing polar functional groups. Therefore, as an organic compound, fluorescence can be easily tuned over visible color by various modifications.

Encouraging from these natural sources, we have prepared red, green, and blue color emissive CDs from zero-cost, bio-degradable, and green sources, i.e., star jasmine leaves (Trachelospermum jasminoides) extract by the solvothermal method. The red and blue colored emissive CDs are achieved in the DMF and water medium, respectively. We used the zinc metal doping in DMF based-extract to prepare green color emissive CDs (G-CDs). The multicolor fluorescence exhibited the maximum emission at 466 nm (blue), 521 nm (green), and 625 nm (red). The extracts of plant leaves in different solvents have distinct content of phytochemicals. At higher temperatures (190 °C) these phytochemicals get carbonized and form a distinct conjugated system with unique surface functional groups. Therefore, in presence of a different solvent or metal doping, CDs show variation in optical properties. Considering their multicolor emitting behavior, we determined that as-synthesized CDs have much potential to act as an excellent color-converter. Fortunately, these CDs have adequate quantum yield and photo-stability, which favored their feasibility for WLED application. Further, the detailed mechanism has been studied for fluorescence tuning by various optical and physicochemical characterization. Besides, our study demonstrated that as-synthesized red emissive CDs have water and chloroform detection abilities for a broad range.

The jasmine leaves were collected from the courtyard of Science Block IIT ISM, Dhanbad. The dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone, ethanol, tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethyl acetate, and chloroform were purchased from Merck India. The sodium hydroxide and zinc acetate were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. India. The silica gel 100–200 mesh for column chromatography was brought from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. India. Distilled water was used throughout the experiment. The double distilled water is used during the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of red, blue, and green CDs
ce:section.ce:para: First, fresh jasmine plant leaves were washed with distilled water and dried at ~30 °C to remove extra water droplets from the leaf surface. Then, 1 g of leaves were dipped in 10 ml of DMF to extract green pigments. The jasmine extract was filtered and transferred into 30 ml of Teflon-lined autoclave, treated at 190 °C for 8 h. After cooling naturally at room temperature, the CDs suspension was centrifuged at 5000 rpm and filtered by Whatman filter paper to remove the untreated raw materials and organic residues. Further, these resultant CDs (R-CDs) were purified by column chromatography using 100–200 mesh size silica gel as a stationary phase and chloroform/ethyl acetate mixture as a mobile phase. The R-CDs were extracted at two different mixtures % of chloroform and ethyl acetate. The R-CDs collected at 5% and 40% chloroform/ethyl acetate mixture was named as R1-CDs and R2-CDs, respectively. For G-CDs, 1 mmol of zinc acetate was added with 10 ml of DMF leaf extract during solvothermal treatment. However, the blue color emissive CDs (B-CDs) were synthesized in a water medium. All the procedures evolved in the synthesis of G-CDs and B-CDs were the same as R-CDs except purification step by column chromatography. The G-CDs and B-CDs were first filtered by Whatman filter paper and then centrifuged at 10,000 RPM. Further, both the CDs were filtered by a 0.22-micron syringe filter.

The polyurethane (PU) solution was made by adding 5 g of PU in 30 ml of DMF by continuous stirring for 6 h under heat at 90 °C. To this solution, 1 ml of each carbon dots were added separately and stirred continuously. These CDs/PU solutions were dropped down on round-shaped plastic material to shape up the polymer composite. After that, the molded CDs/PU solution was vacuum dried at 50 °C, resulting R2-CDs/PU, G-CDs/PU, and B-CDs/PU. The PU was selected since R2-CDs and G-CDs were synthesized in DMF such that they show similar emissions as bare CDs.

White light-emissive mixture was prepared from R2-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs. By varying the RGB (Red, Green, and Blue) dots proportion in PU solution, white light-emissive polymer solution and films were prepared. The excitation wavelength was 370 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of organic solvents on R2-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The R2-CDs were mixed with various organic solvents including, acetone, ethyl acetate, ethanol, chloroform, and THF. The fluorescence images were captured by adding 100 µL R2-CDs in each of these solvents, individually. For the visual detection and LOD calculation, various concentration (0–80, V/V %) of chloroform and water were prepared with R2-CDs, separately. The photoluminescence spectra were recorded at 400 nm excitation. All the images were captured using the normal mobile camera in presence of a UV lamp under 365 nm excitation.

The UV–VIS absorbance spectra were measured by Perkin-Elmer 950 USA spectrometer. The PL excitation and emission spectrum were tested using Perkin Elmer LS 55 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The Fourier Transformed Infrared (FTIR) spectrum was used to scan the surface functional groups using Agilent Cary 660 instrument. A Versa Probe II PHI 5000 system was tested the X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) to further confirm the surface functionalization. The particle size of carbon dots was measured using Talos F200X G2 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM). The Lab Ram HR Evolution Raman spectrometer of Horiba Scientific is used to check the Raman shift. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) were done by Perkin Elmer Thermogravimetric Analyzer 4000 instrument at 10 °C/min. The JOBIN-VYON M/S system was measured the nanosecond fluorescence lifetime decay. The image of fluorescent carbon dots was taken using a UV lamp under 365 nm excitation. The LED parameters i.e., color rendering index (CRI), color-coordinate (CIE) and correlated color temperature (CCT) were calculated with Color calculator software.

The synthesis of multicolor emissive CDs from a natural source is an exciting area of research. Such bio-inspired green precursors materials are the sustainable and developed source of conjugated carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen system. Apart from using in bioimaging, sensing, and photocatalytic degradation as given in Table 1, here naturally derived CDs are applied in WLEDs fabrication. WLEDs fabricated from natural precursors will aspire to bring the most eco-friendly lights. For this purpose, RGB color emissive CDs are synthesized using star jasmine leaves. Star jasmine leaves are a major source of ascorbic acid, salicylic acid, anthranilic acid, indole oxygenase, and alkaloid jasminine. The presence of highly conjugated acid and amine system in jasmine leaves motivated us for fluorescence tuning by various modifications. The red color emissive CDs are synthesized in the DMF medium. Solvent alteration way of surface modification is used to produce blue color emissive CDs. A metal doping approach is assisted to prepare green color emissive CDs. Fig. 1 represents the synthesis scheme of RGB CDs.

The optical properties are studied by UV–VIS absorbance and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The UV–VIS absorbance, photoluminescence excitation (PLEx), and photoluminescence emission (PLEm) of RGB CDs are shown in Fig. 2. The CDs synthesized in the DMF medium illustrate fluorescence emission at 430 nm, 625 nm, and 656 nm as Fig. S1a -b represents their PLEm, PLEx, and absorbance spectrum. A column chromatographic way of purification is used to collect these red-region fluorophores. After the column chromatography, R1-CDs and R2-CDs are collected, showing emissions at 656 nm and 625 nm, respectively. The R2-CDs show absorbance band at 276 & 306 nm due to π-π* transition (CC) [33] and sharp peak at 403 nm due to n-π* transition (CN/CO) (Fig. 2a) [17]. Fig. 2b shows the excitation-independent fluorescence emission at 625 nm. Moreover, the R1-CDs display the maximum absorbance at around 410 nm and emission at 656 nm (Fig. S1c-d). The quantum yield of R2-CDs is 19.8% compared to the Rhodamine 6 G (95%, H2O), much higher than R1-CDs (Fig. S2). Therefore, we have preferred R2-CDs for further analysis and experiments. The UV–VIS absorbance and fluorescence emission of unreacted leaf extract is studied to verify the formation of CDs (Fig. S3a-b). Compared with unreacted leaf extract, R2-CDs have a lesser and more prominent absorbance peak, originating a more conjugated system [34]. Other than that, a shifting (20–50 nm) in fluorescence emission is also perceived. The calculated QY of untreated leaf extract is 15.6% (Fig. S4), which is lesser than R2-CDs. Many literatures have already reported higher photostability of CDs than untreated extract [27]. Similarly, the as-synthesized RGB CDs don’t show a dramatic change in fluorescence spectra for six months (Fig. S5) and under UV light irradiation (Fig. S6), demonstrating long-term stability. Further, TGA and DTG of RGB CDs have examined in nitrogen atmosphere, shown in Fig. S7. We can infer the superiority of synthesized CDs over untreated leaf extract for WLEDs application. Along with red CDs, green and blue color emissive CDs are highly required. Metal doping is one method to modify the CDs chemical functionality in order to shift their PL emission. Here, Zn metal ion doping is applied to tune the PL emission. After the Zn metal doping, λmax is red-shifted and shows maximum absorbance at 456 nm (Fig. 2c). The shifting in UV absorbance supports the metal doping within the carbogenic surface [35]. The PLEx and PLEm are at 457 and 521 nm, respectively (Fig. 2d). The G-CDs show excitation-independent PLEm and fluorescence shifting (˃100 nm)(> 100 nm) compared to the CDs without metal doping. The reason behind this excitation-independent emission and PL shifting is attributed to the incorporation of Zn metal [36]. In order to attain blue color emission, the solvent tailoring approach is applied to tune the PL emission of CDs. By changing the reaction medium from DMF to water, CDs show blue shifting in absorbance and fluorescence emission. Fig. 2e demonstrates that B-CDs display two absorbance peaks at 280 and 330 nm, assigned to the π-π* of CC and n-π* of CO/CN. As increasing the excitation wavelength from 340 to 370 nm, the PL intensity increases, showing maximum emission at 466 nm (Fig. 2f). The untreated leaf extract in the water medium does not exhibit any fluorescence emission. Probably, the minimum extraction of red emissive phytochemicals occurs in a water medium. Though, the extraction of pigments and their stability is much higher in DMF [37]. DMF is a richer source of nitrogen and can well act as a solvent as well as dopants. Many researchers have claimed the presence of phytochemicals in the extract of leaves like alkaloids, pseudotanins, saponins, steroids, flavonoids, resins, glycosides, cardiac glycosides, carbohydrates, phenols, etc. They revealed that the leaf extract in various solvents shows positive and negative results based on phytochemical tests [38]. Based on these results, it is inferred that at higher temperatures (190 °C) the phytochemicals get carbonized and formed a more conjugated system. Therefore, the presence of different phytochemicals in various solvents results in different optical properties. The plot of fluorescence integrated area and UV–VIS absorbance are given in Fig. S8a-b. The QY of B-CDs and G-CDs is 54.8% and 15%. The Quinine sulfate (55%, 0.1 M H2SO4) and fluorescein (95%, 0.1 M NaOH) are chosen as reference dyes for B-CDs and G-CDs, respectively.

The size and morphology of RGB CDs are explored by TEM analysis. Fig. 3a-c shows that RGB CDs have a spherical morphology and the d-f histogram displays an average particle size of R-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs is 4.06 nm, 3.15 nm, and 2.07 nm, respectively. The fluorescence emission of RGB CDs is highly related to their particle size, demonstrating that the RGB CDs obey the quantum size effect. In our case, we found that the red emissive CDs have the largest particle size. It is signifying that solvent and metal doping modification approaches control the particle size. The high-resolution TEM image of R2-CDs shows the clear lattice fringe of 0.32 nm for the (002) lattice plane of graphite [39] (Fig. S9a), representing the crystalline nature of CDs [25]. The XRD also confirmed the existence of the (002) plane as the Fig. S9b evidenced a diffraction peak at 19.3°. The lattice fringe of R2-CDs (0.32 nm) is similar to the graphitic interlayer spacing (0.33 nm) with some broadness in the spectra [40]. Lattice fringes are not observed in the case of G-CDs and B-CDs.

The identification of surface functional groups is carried out by FTIR. Fig. 4a shows the FTIR spectra of R2-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs. The RGB CDs display broad absorption peak at 3300–3500 cm−1, which is assigned to the vibration of OH and NH bond [41]. The symmetrical and asymmetrical vibration of C-H are specified at 2925 cm−1 and 2857 cm−1 [42]. B-CDs display prominent peak for 1668 cm−1 and 1595 cm−1, attributed for CO and CC bond [42]. Although, G-CDs show broader absorption spectrum at 1604 cm−1 indicating the overlapping of CO and CC bond. B-CDs and G-CDs have characteristic peak at 1395 cm−1 and 1110 cm−1, which is ascribed for stretching vibration of C-O. The presence of CO, CO, and OH bond specifies the presence of COOH groups on B-CDs and G-CDs surface. Remarkably, R2-CDs have additional peak at 1728 cm−1 for conjugated aromatic ester/amide containing CO bond [43]; 1250 cm−1 & 1160 cm−1 for asymmetrical stretching vibration of C-O ester groups [44]. These peaks confirm the formation of ester (-COOR) and amide (-CONR) in R2-CDs. Under high temperature (190 °C), all the phytochemical gets oxidized or modified their surface during carbonization. The introduction or formation of various oxygen and nitrogen containing groups can easily tune the oxidation state, and simultaneously their optical properties. The red emission occurs possibly due to formation of highly conjugated -COOR and -CONR system. And the lower wavelength emission appears due to the presence of carboxylic moieties on their surface. This is apparent by difference in the IR spectra of R2-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs. Further, to confirm this mechanism, XPS is analyzed. The XPS showed strong peak for C1s (~287 eV), N1s (~401 eV), and O1s (~533 eV) for RGB CDs (Fig. 4b). The elemental composition of each CDs is given in Table S1. The R2-CDs is highly enriched with carbon and have least N/C and O/C elemental content. This is similar with the high-resolution XPS of C1s as R2-CDs (Fig. S10a) contains C-C/CC (284.37 eV) structure extremely and C-O/C-N (287.29 eV) in very lesser amount. Fig. S10b shows the O1s high-resolution spectra have two binding sites at 529.44 eV (CO) and 531.59 eV (C-O). The N1s is constituted by only pyridinic-N, representing the formation CNC bond (Fig. S9c). It is signifying that the R2-CDs have extensive conjugated carbonic system with some -COOR and -CONR moieties on their surface. For G-CDs and B-CDs, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s are constituted by C-C/CC, C-O/C-N, and COOH (Fig. S10d and g) [45]. Though, G-CDs showed slight shift in peak position. It may be due to change in local chemical environment because of metal ion. Fig. S10e and h exhibits the deconvolution peak for O1s of G-CDs and B-CDs, displaying CO and C-OH groups. It is displaying that B-CDs and G-CDs have rich carboxylic groups, which is similar as IR. Consequently, N1s (Fig. S10f) is constituted with pyridinic-N and pyrrolic/pyridonic-N in G-CDs. Although, B-CDs is constituted by pyridinic-N, pyrrolic/pyridonic-N, and quaternary-N (Fig. S10i) [46]. The G-CDs have additional peak at 1024 and 1047 for core state of Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2 [47]. It is specified that Zn species is in divalent state as the energy separation between these two-oxidation state is 23 eV [47]. Additionally, G-CDs have peak at 13 eV, 91 eV, and 141 eV for different oxidation state of Zn. The existence of Zn LMM binding energy in G-CDs, indicating the presence of Zn in higher oxidation state. Interestingly, Zn LMM auger lines are found at 501 eV, signifying the presence of ZnO bond [48]. This is conveying that some Zn combined with carboxyl groups and replace the carboxylic hydrogen. Some Zn species combined with two carboxylic groups and present in OZnO bond. The remaining Zn ion is adsorbed on the surface of CDs. It means that initially Zn reduces the hydroxyl and further the carbonyl bond on the CDs surface [48,49]. The presence of CO is crucial for surface passivation as well as for PLQY. Therefore, the G-CDs have lower PLQY than R2-CDs and B-CDs. It is well reported that the addition or doping of Zn metal lowers the PLQY of CDs [49]. In G-CDs, Zn binds with carboxylic moieties, and the formation of conjugated ester/bond is excluded. In lack of extended conjugating system with ester/amide bond, G-CDs originate the green color emission. This is consistent with IR and XPS.

Further, Raman is also used to verify this extent of disorder and graphitization. In Raman, D band is attributed for defects or disorder and G band is for graphitic carbon.
Fig. 5a-c displays the intensity Id/Ig ratio of R2-, G-, B-CDs is 0.80, 1.07, and 0.90, respectively and follows the order as R2-CDs<B-CD<G-CDs. It is representing that the doping of Zn decreases the extent of graphitization in G-CDs. Simultaneously, the RGB CDs display an additional peak for CH2/CH3 bending [50], which is consistent with IR stretching.

To clarify the PL origin, time-correlated fluorescence analysis of these RGB dots is performed. The RGB CDs is fitted triple-exponentially based on the following Eq. (1).
(1)
Y
t
=
b
1
e
−
t
τ
1
+
b
2
e
−
t
τ
2
+
b
3
e
−
t
τ
3

The lifetime decay is constituted by τ1, τ2, and τ3; , where τ1 is assigned for electronic transition in core state; τ2 and τ3 is attributed for transition in edge and surface state [51]. And, b1, b2, and b3 are the fitting parameter for τ1, τ2, and τ3 time, respectively. The tri-exponential fitting of as-synthesized RGB CDs is signifying that the luminescence originates from carbon core, edge, and surface state. Fig. 5d-f shows the average lifetime of R2-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs is 3.66 ns, 7.87 ns, and 5.36 ns, respectively. In decay kinetics, R2-CDs have the least contribution for the τ1 and the most contribution for τ2 and τ3, signifying τ2 and τ3 as the dominant factor. The τ2 and τ3 as short-lived components and more area contribution of B2 and B3 is indicating that the low decay kinetics are mainly happened due to edge/surface state in R2-CDs. It is signifying that the fluorescence lifetime in R2-CDs is mostly determined by carbon core. Whereas, in G-CDs and B-CDs, the long-lived component is τ2 and τ3. Therefore, the lifetime of G-CDs and B-CDs is determined by τ2 and τ3, representing the surface defect state as the fluorescence emission center. This result indicates that the fluorescence emission in R2-CDs is derived from the quantum size effect, while G-CDs and B-CDs PL emission are derived from both the surface defect state and quantum size effect. Further, we derive a conclusion regarding a better lifetime in G-CDs. The presence of the core state as a dominant factor in G-CDs is one of the reasons for higher lifetime decay. The chemical adsorption or surface passivation is the main reason behind longer lifetime as it makes excited state energy traps on CDs surface [52]. Mechanistically, the doping of Zn metal ions enhanced the CDs surface passivation by combining inorganic metal ion and organic functional groups of CDs. The improved surface passivation is causing the increment in lifetime decay in G-CDs. The formation and effect of O-Zn-O is already studied by XPS, which is consistent with the lifetime decay findings. The contribution of τ1 follows the order G-CDs>B-CDs>R2-CDs. Inversely, τ2 and τ3 both follows the order G-CDs<B-CDs<R2-CDs. Consistent with the above, average lifetime decay (τav) follows the order G-CDs>B-CDs>R2-CDs. The % contribution (B1) for τ1 is directly proportional to the τ av . The τ1 is increasing in the multiple of ~1.96 from R2-CDs to B-CDs to G-CDs. And, the average lifetime rises in the multiple of ~1.46 from R2-CDs to B-CDs to G-CDs. The same multiple values from dot-to-dot verifies the linear relationship of τ1 with τ av . Based on this, it is concluded that the core state is the most affecting factor in lifetime decay kinetics.

Recently, WLEDs based on CDs have come into consideration because of their low cost and environment-friendly precursors. More interestingly, CDs synthesized from the green precursors will be the cheapest material for LEDs fabrication. The as-synthesized CDs from jasmine leaves have a great potential to be used as color converters as they have excellent fluorescence emission. The CDs polymer matrix is prepared to sustain its fluorescence stability for a longer time. The polymer composite prevents the CDs from being environmentally contaminated as well as aggregation-induced quenching emission in solid-state. The synthesized RGB CDs have high stability in the DMF medium. Therefore, polyurethane (PU) is selected to prepare the CDs/Polymer composite and films too. It is worth observing that the addition of stabilizer, curing agent, and dispersant are excluded. The polymer film of RGB CDs showed similar fluorescence emission as in solution, shown in Fig. S11. The RGB CDs polymer films show high stability under UV irradiation, as shown in Fig. S12. Fig. 6a-d displays the CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinate of RGB CDs polymer composites and white color emissive polymer composite. Fig. 6e-h shows their respective fluorescence spectrum. The CIE coordinate of monochromatic red, green, and blue color polymer composite is (0.58,0.31), (0.26,0.60), and (0.15,0.18), respectively. The white color is observed by mixing red, green, and blue color emissive dots in 0.5:1:2 vol in PU solution. The LEDs parameter including CIE and CCT is observed for polymer composite. The CCT is 4283 K and the CIE coordinate is (0.36,0.32). Table S2 shows the comparison of as-prepared white LEDs with previously reported organic precursors-based WLEDs. The CIE coordinate is in good agreement with pure white light coordinates (0.33, 0.33). The CCT between 3500 K and 5100 K is considered as natural or neutral white, signifying its potential application in living spaces. The monochromatic LEDs is fabricated by covering the individual color polymer film on white color emissive LEDs. Fig. 6i-l shows the Blue-, Green-, Red-, and white emissive LEDs. The WLEDs is observed by covering the RGB polymer film on UV LEDs, enlarged image is shown in Fig. S13 .

To check the effect of different solvents on the R2-CDs optical properties, the fluorescence spectrum is analyzed in acetone, ethyl acetate, ethanol, chloroform, and THF. Fig. S14 shows the PLEm and visual detection image of R2-CDs in these solvents. This is evident that the red color of R2-CDs is decayed in water and chloroform medium (not observed) only. Therefore, we assume that this R2-CDs can be used as water and chloroform sensors. To facilitate this, 0–80 (%, V/V) of chloroform and water is prepared with DMF individually. The change specifies over a broad concentration range 0–80 (%, V/V) of water as well as in chloroform as shown in Fig. 7. The observable color change can be perceived visually at 10 (%, V/V) chloroform and 20 (%, V/V) water, respectively (Fig. S15). By increasing the concentration of chloroform and water, the red color of R2-CDs is getting faded. It is noted that the best sensitivity is observed at 10–40%, V/V. For chloroform and water, the linear regression equation is presented as F/F0 = 0.745–0.005 C and F/F0 = 1.015–0.0064 C respectively, where C is signified for DMF concentration. The LOD of chloroform and water is calculated based on 3σ/K, where σ is expressed for the standard deviation of intercept and K is for the slope of fitted line. The σ is calculated by the given Eq. (2).

where S.E. is the standard error of intercept and N is the number of the data point. The LOD of chloroform and water is 2.21% and 0.907%, respectively. Further, the fluorescence quenching mechanism is determined by measuring the UV–VIS absorbance and PL lifetime (Fig. S16). By increasing the concentration of chloroform and water, the UV–VIS absorbance spectra decrease parallel to the fluorescence emission, signifying the static mechanism in the quenching process [53,54]. The PL lifetime of R2-CDs is 3.66 ns without the addition of solvent (water or chloroform). While, the average lifetime of R2-CDs decreased dramatically to 2.07 ns and 1.50 ns at 60% V/V water and chloroform concentration, respectively. The PL lifetime of R2-CDs decayed in presence of solvent (water or chloroform), indicating the dynamic quenching mechanism [54,55]. In this case, R2-CDs exhibit both the static and dynamic quenching mechanism in presence of solvent (water and chloroform). The main advantage is its broader range detection visibly as well as fluorometrically, which make this a potential solvent sensor to perform detection on spot.

In brief, we developed optically tuned CDs from green precursor Star Jasmine leaves. The attractiveness of these as-synthesized RGB CDs is that they covered full visible color and showed emission at 466 nm, 521 nm, and 625 nm. Evaluating the decay kinetics, we found that the carbon core state decay (τ1) is directly proportional to the average lifetime decay (τav). Further, the photostable emission properties of these green fluorophores direct its application in LEDs. The CIE coordinate of monochromatic red, green, and blue color polymer composite is (0.58,0.31), (0.26,0.60), and (0.15,0.18), respectively. For WLEDs, the CCT and CIE coordinates are 4283 K and (0.36,0.32), imported its potential application in living spaces. In addition to this, R2-CDs act like water and chloroform detectors visibly as well as fluorometrically. Such types of highly stable green CDs will pave their working window in the LEDs application. Further findings of more such green precursors could unveil an immense future in the LEDs field.
Rinki Kumari: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Sumanta Kumar Sahu: Visualization, Investigation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: R. Kumari is obliged to the Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad for awarding research fellowship. We would like to acknowledge the Central Research Facility, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM) for HR TEM, XPS, XRD, and Raman analysis. R. Kumari is also thankful to the Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility IIT Madras for the fluorescence lifetime decay analysis.

New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wei, Y. Yuan, H. Li, D. Hao, C. Sun, G. Zheng, R. Wang, A novel fluorescent sensor for water in organic solvents based on dynamic quenching of carbon quantum dots, New J. Chem., 42(2018), pp. 18787–18793

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive quantum dots-based “OFF-ON” fluorescent sensor for ruthenium anticancer drugs and ctDNA
Colloids Surf. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Huang, F. Zhu, H. Qiu, Q. Xiao, Q. Zhou, W. Su, B. Hu, A sensitive quantum dots-based “OFF-ON” fluorescent sensor for ruthenium anticancer drugs and ctDNA, Colloids Surf., B, 117(2014), pp. 240–247
